15: spoudason seauton dokimon parastēsai to theo ergaten anepaiskunton orthotomounta ton logon tes aletheias (spoudason seauton dokimon parastēsai tō theō, ergatēn anepaiskunton, orthotomounta ton logon tēs alētheias): 'Do your best to present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who has no need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth.' Orthotomounta (rightly dividing/handling/cutting): used in LXX Proverbs 3:6 ('he will make straight your paths') and 2 Timothy 11:5. The word evokes a craftsman cutting or laying material in a straight line — a road-builder, a carpenter, a surgeon. The image: the handling of the word of truth requires the precision and skill of a craftsman who cuts straight rather than crooked. Historically, the King James 'rightly dividing' was applied dispensationally; the original metaphor is about competent, accurate, straight-course exposition.
1: Endynamou en tē chariti tē en Christō Iēsou (be strengthened in the grace that is in Christ Jesus): endynamoō (present passive imperative) — to be continuously empowered/strengthened, with the passive implying divine enabling. The source of strength is grace — the unearned gift-relationship in Christ — rather than Timothy's own resources. The opening imperative of 2 Timothy 2 answers the preceding chapters' portrait of Paul's suffering: Timothy is not left to find strength in himself but is directed to the same grace that sustains Paul.
2: Ha ēkousas par' emou dia pollōn martyrōn, tauta parathes pistois anthrōpois, hoitines hikanoi esontai kai heterous didaxai (what you heard from me through many witnesses, entrust these things to faithful people who will also be qualified to teach others): the apostolic tradition-transmission chain in one verse: Paul → Timothy → faithful people → others (four generations). Dia pollōn martyrōn (through many witnesses) — the instruction passed through many witnesses makes Timothy's knowledge both publicly verified and communally accountable. Parathes (entrust, deposit) — paratithēmi, the same root as parathēkē (deposit); the tradition-transmission is another act of deposit-entrustment.
3: Synkakopathēson hōs kalos stratiotēs Christou Iēsou (suffer together as a good soldier of Christ Jesus): the second use of synkakopatheo (2 Timothy 1:8; 2 Timothy 2:3) — sharing in bad/evil suffering as a compound act. The first of three metaphors in 2 Timothy 2:3-6 (soldier, athlete, farmer), all pointing to the necessity of disciplined endurance before reward. Kalos stratiotēs (good soldier) — the military metaphor was common in Stoic and Cynic discourse for the philosopher who endures hardship.
4: Oudeis strateuomenos empleketai tais tou biou pragmateiais (no one serving as a soldier gets entangled in civilian affairs): emplekō = to entangle, interweave; the soldier who is entangled in civilian business (pragmateiai) fails to please his commanding officer. Stratologēsanti (to the one who recruited/enlisted him) — the participle describes the commanding officer who selected and enrolled the soldier. The application to ministry: ministerial focus requires freedom from civilian entanglements. This was a real issue — first-century artisans in ministry might be tempted to combine their trade with itinerant teaching in ways that compromised both.
5: Ean de kai athlē tis, ou stephanoūtai ean mē nomimōs athlēsē (but if anyone competes as an athlete, he does not receive the crown unless he competes lawfully): athlē (competes as an athlete, strives in the contest) — the verb is rare; athlos = contest, struggle. Stephanoō (to crown) — the victor's wreath at the great games; the image connects to 2 Timothy 4:8 (the crown of righteousness). Nomimōs (lawfully, according to the rules) — competitors who violated the rules of the games (diet requirements, legitimate birth, rule-following in the contest itself) were disqualified regardless of performance. Lawful conduct precedes the crown.
6: Ton kopiōnta geōrgon dei prōton tōn karpōn metalambanein (the hard-working farmer should be first to receive a share of the crops): kopiaō (to toil exhaustingly) — Paul's standard term for his own apostolic labor (1 Corinthians 15:10; Galatians 4:11; Philippians 2:16; Colossians 1:29); the kopiōnta geōrgon (toiling farmer) is another image for the minister who labors to exhaustion. Dei prōton metalambanein (should be first to receive) — the laborer has a right to benefit from the harvest; the same principle applies in 1 Timothy 5:17-18 (the elder worthy of double honor).
7: Noei ho legō (understand/reflect on what I am saying): the brief direct address invites Timothy to do the exegetical work of applying the three metaphors himself. Dōsei gar soi ho Kyrios synesin en pasin (for the Lord will give you understanding in all things) — the divine grant of understanding answers the human exercise of reflection; understanding is both sought (reflect on this) and given (the Lord grants it). Synesis (understanding, comprehension) — the capacity to bring together disparate pieces into a coherent grasp; here it is a gift of the Lord.
8: Mnēmoneue Iēsoun Christon egēgermenon ek nekrōn (remember Jesus Christ raised from the dead): the present imperative mnēmoneue (keep remembering, hold in memory) — not a one-time recall but continuous memory. The object of memory is specified in its most theologically dense form: Jesus Christ, raised from the dead, from the seed of David. Ek spermatos Dauid (from the seed of David) — the Davidic lineage anchors the resurrection claim in covenantal-historical promise; the raised Messiah is the promised Davidic heir. Kata to euangelion mou (according to my gospel) — Paul's personalized formula for the core proclamation.
9: Hēn kakourgos dēsmiōs (for which I suffer as a criminal in chains): kakourgos = evildoer, criminal — the same Greek word used for the criminals crucified alongside Jesus (Luke 23:32-33, 39). Paul occupies the same social position as the criminals of the crucifixion narrative: chained as a criminal in Roman custody. All' ho logos tou theou ou dedetai (but the word of God is not chained) — the contrast between the imprisoned apostle and the unchained word: Paul's physical constraints cannot limit the gospel's movement. The word goes where Paul cannot.
10: Panta hypomenō dia tous eklektous (I endure all things for the sake of the elect): hypomenō (to endure under, remain under) — the compound of remaining under a burden rather than fleeing it. Tous eklektous (the elect) — Paul's suffering has an ecclesial and eschatological purpose: the elect who will obtain salvation through the gospel proclaimed through Paul's ministry. Sōtērias tychōsin tēs en Christō Iēsou meta doxēs aiōniou (may obtain salvation in Christ Jesus with eternal glory) — tynchanō = to happen upon, attain; the elect "happen upon" salvation through the proclamation that Paul's endurance makes possible.
11: Pistos ho logos (faithful/trustworthy is the word): the Pastoral Epistles' formula for introducing a pre-formed saying or hymn fragment (1 Timothy 1:15; 2 Timothy 3:1; 2 Timothy 4:9; Titus 3:8). What follows in 2 Timothy 2:11-13 is a tightly structured four-line antithetical series: death/life; endurance/reign; disowning/disowned; faithlessness/faithfulness. Ei gar synapethanomen, kai syzēsomen (for if we died with him, we will also live with him) — the first condition applies the baptismal death-burial-resurrection pattern (Romans 6:4-8) to eschatological hope; dying with Christ grounds living with Christ.
12: Ei hypomenomen, kai symbasileusomen (if we endure, we will also reign with him): the endurance-reigning pair extends the death-life parallel; faithful endurance now leads to co-reign later (cf. Revelation 20:4). Ei arnēsometha, kakeinōs arnēsetai hēmas (if we disown him, he will also disown us): the warning echoes Jesus's logion (Matthew 10:33; Luke 12:9) — the one who denies Christ before people will be denied before the Father. The mutual-denial clause is the sharpest point in the series, directed at the pressure Timothy faces to disown the imprisoned Paul.
13: Ei apistoumen, ekeinos pistos menei (if we are faithless, he remains faithful): the fourth and theologically climactic clause breaks the symmetry — unlike the previous three, this one does not mirror the human action in the divine response. Apistountes (faithless) and pistos menei (remains faithful) — God's faithfulness is not contingent on human faithfulness. Arnēsasthai gar heauton ou dynatai (for he cannot deny himself) — the ground of his unfailing faithfulness: self-denial is ontologically impossible for the self-consistent God. This clause functions as the theological foundation for the entire hymn-fragment: the asymmetry between human inconstancy and divine constancy.
14: Tauta hypomimnēske, diamartyromenos enōpion tou theou (remind them of these things, solemnly charging before God): hypomimnēskō (to remind by bringing to mind) — the task of pastoral memory-management; the congregation needs continuous reminding of established truth. Mē logomachein (not to quarrel about words/engage in word-battles) — logomachia (word-battle) appears also in 1 Timothy 6:4; the quarrel-about-words is a recognized vice of false teachers; it produces nothing useful (ep' ouden chrēsimon) and subverts the hearers (epi katastrophē tōn akouontōn).
16: Tas de bebēlous kenophōnias periistaso (but avoid godless empty chatter): periistēmi (to stand around, avoid, shun) — the avoidance is active evasion. Bebēlos kenophōnia (godless empty-chatter) — bebēlos = profane, accessible to anyone (not restricted to the holy); kenophōnia = empty-sounding speech, void of content. Prokopsousin gar epi pleion asebeias (for they will advance further into ungodliness) — the empty chatter doesn't stay static but progresses; prokoptō (to cut forward, advance) is used ironically: they advance, but toward greater ungodliness rather than toward truth.
17: Gangraina hexei (will have/spread like gangrene): gangraina (gangrene) — the spreading necrosis of tissue; the medical metaphor is precise: false teaching, like gangrene, spreads from the infected tissue to healthy tissue, destroying community health from within. Hymenaeus kai Philētos — Hymenaeus appears also in 1 Timothy 1:20 (handed over to Satan); Philetus is otherwise unknown. The naming of specific individuals is a characteristic feature of the Pastoral Epistles' polemical sections — the error has real, identifiable proponents.
18: Hoi peri tēn alētheian ēstochēsan, legontes tēn anastasin ēdē gegonenai (who missed the mark about the truth, saying the resurrection has already happened): ēstocheō (to miss the mark, fail to hit the target) — the error is not ignorance but misdirection; they are shooting but missing. The specific error — anastasin ēdē gegonenai (the resurrection has already taken place) — is a form of over-realized eschatology; it absorbs the future bodily resurrection into a present spiritual event (possibly baptismal death-and-resurrection experienced as total fulfillment). This denial destroys (anatrepousin = to overturn, upset) the faith of some.
19: Ho mentoi stereos themelios tou theou hestēken (nevertheless the solid foundation of God stands firm): stereos (solid, firm, hard) contrasts with the collapsing faith of those overthrown by the error. The foundation is God's foundation — not the false teachers' construction. Echōn tēn sphragida tautēn (having this seal/inscription) — a foundation stone with an inscription was a known feature of significant public buildings. The two inscriptions are: (1) Egnō Kyrios tous ontas autou (the Lord knows those who are his) — echoing Numbers 16:5 LXX (God knows those who belong to him; YHWH's recognition of his own in the Korah rebellion); and (2) Apostētō apo adikias pas ho onomazōn to onoma Kyriou (let everyone who names the Lord's name depart from unrighteousness) — the positive corollary.
20: En megalē de oikia ouk estin monon skeuē chrysa kai argyra alla kai xylina kai ostrakina (in a large house there are not only gold and silver vessels but also wood and clay): the oikia (great house) metaphor for the church or the divine household; the vessels (skeuē) range from precious (chrysa, argyra) to common (xylina, ostrakina). Ha men eis timēn ha de eis atimian (some for honor and some for dishonor) — the distinction between honorable and dishonorable purposes. The metaphor raises the question of whether the vessels are differentiated by divine selection or by their own purification — 2 Timothy 2:21 answers the latter.
21: Ean oun tis ekkatharē heauton apo toutōn (if anyone cleanses himself from these things): the reflexive heauton (himself) places the initiative for purification with the individual; the passive vessel becomes active in self-cleansing. Estai skeuos eis timēn (he will be a vessel for honor) — the shift from passive vessel to active purifier to honored vessel tracks the movement from contamination through decision to usefulness. Hēgiasmenon, euchrēston tō despotē, eis pan ergon agathon hētoimasmenon (sanctified, useful to the master, prepared for every good work) — three qualities of the honored vessel: consecrated, serviceable, and ready.
22: Tas neoterkas epithumias pheuge (flee youthful desires): neoterikos (youthful, belonging to youth) — the desires specifically associated with youth: impetuosity, passion for novelty, love of argument, sexual desire. Pheuge (flee) — the same verb as 1 Timothy 6:11 (flee these things); active flight rather than passive resistance. The four virtues to pursue — dikaiosynē, pistin, agapēn, eirēnēn (righteousness, faith, love, peace) — are communal: pursued meta tōn epikaloumonōn ton Kyrion ek katharas kardias (with those who call on the Lord from a pure heart); virtue is not a solo achievement.
23: Tas de mōras kai apaideutos zētēseis paraitou (but refuse/decline foolish and uneducated speculations): mōros kai apaideutos (foolish and undisciplined/uneducated) — the speculations are critiqued on two grounds: intellectual (foolish, without wisdom) and formational (undisciplined, without the formation of genuine education). Paraiteomai (to decline, excuse oneself from, refuse) — the verb of polite but firm refusal; the minister who engages the controversies legitimizes them. Gennōsin machas (they generate quarrels) — the output of foolish speculation is not light but conflict.
24: Doulos de Kyriou ou dei machesthai alla ēpion einai pros pantas (the Lord's servant must not be quarrelsome but gentle toward all): doulos Kyriou (servant/slave of the Lord) — the OT title (ebed YHWH) applied to Moses (Deuteronomy 34:5), Joshua (Joshua 24:29), and David (Psalms 89:3); its application to the Christian minister places him in the prophetic-leadership succession. Ēpios (gentle, mild, kind) — the quality of the nursing mother in 1 Thessalonians 2:7; the antithesis of contentious. Didaktikon (able to teach) and anexikakos (patient under evil, tolerant) complete the portrait of the minister who absorbs opposition rather than reflecting it.
25: En prautēti paideuonta tous antitithemenous (gently instructing those who oppose): prautēs (gentleness, meekness) — one of the Spirit's fruit (Galatians 5:23); the minister who instructs opponents in gentleness rather than confrontation. Mēpote dōē autois ho theos metanoian eis epignōsin alētheias (whether God might grant them repentance into knowledge of the truth) — the optative mēpote dōē expresses hope rather than certainty; repentance is a divine grant, not a human achievement. Epignōsis (full/precise knowledge) — the intensive compound in the Pastoral Epistles marks genuine knowledge of truth as the goal toward which repentance leads.
26: Ananēpsōsin ek tēs tou diabolou pagidos (that they may come to their senses out of the devil's trap): ananēphō = to become sober again, to recover one's senses (as from intoxication or sleep) — the hapax image is of those who have been mentally captured requiring an awakening to reality. Pagis tou diabolou (trap of the devil) — the hunting snare; the opponents are simultaneously captors and captives: taken captive by the devil (ezōgrēmenoi hyp' autou) to do his will (eis to ekeinon thelēma). The ambiguous pronoun ekeinon (his) refers most naturally to the devil: captured to do the devil's will.
